title: How to Get Your Accident Police Report
metaTitle: How to Get Your Accident Police Report — CA and AZ
metaDescription: Your police report is essential for any insurance claim. Learn how to get it in California or Arizona, what it contains, and how to correct any errors.
description: The police report from your accident is one of the most important documents in any insurance claim or lawsuit. It is the official record created by a neutral third party — law enforcement — and it contains facts, witness information, and sometimes a preliminary fault determination. Knowing how to get it, read it, and correct any errors can meaningfully affect your claim.

sections.heading: Why the Police Report Matters
sections.content: Insurance companies use the police report as a foundational document when evaluating claims. It establishes the basic facts of the accident — date, time, location, parties, vehicles, and observed conditions — from a neutral source. A fault notation by the responding officer, while not legally binding, carries weight in claim negotiations. The report also identifies witnesses who were present but may be harder to locate later. Having your own copy ensures you can verify that the information is accurate and challenge any errors that could harm your claim. You should obtain and read your police report before your first substantive conversation with any insurance adjuster.
tips: Get the report number at the scene so you can request the full report quickly
Insurance adjusters will obtain and review the police report — you should read it first
Witness names in the police report can be used to gather supporting statements later

sections.heading: How to Get Your Police Report in California
sections.content: In California, police reports are handled by the agency that responded to the accident — California Highway Patrol (CHP), the local police department, or the county sheriff. CHP reports can be requested online through the CHP website, by mail, or in person at the responding CHP office. Most CHP reports are available within 10 days of the accident. Local police department reports are requested directly from the department's records division. The fee is typically $10 to $25. Bring your case or event number, the date of the accident, and valid identification. Processing times vary by agency — call ahead to confirm availability.
tips: CHP reports: request online at sar.chp.ca.gov or at the responding CHP office
Local police accidents: contact the responding department's records division directly
Allow 7 to 14 days from the accident date before requesting — reports take time to finalize

sections.heading: How to Get Your Police Report in Arizona
sections.content: In Arizona, accident reports are filed by the responding agency — the Arizona Department of Public Safety (DPS) for highway accidents, or the local police or sheriff for city and county roads. DPS reports can be requested online through the Arizona DPS Crash Report Portal, by mail, or in person. Local agency reports are requested through the records divisions of the corresponding department. Typical fees are $5 to $15. Reports are generally available within 7 to 10 business days of the accident. Arizona law requires drivers to file their own report if law enforcement did not file one for accidents involving injury or damage over $1,000.
tips: AZ DPS crash reports: available through the AZ DPS online portal or by mail
If police did not respond, Arizona drivers must file their own report for accidents involving injury or damage over $1,000
Reports typically take 7 to 10 business days to be finalized and available

sections.heading: What the Police Report Contains
sections.content: A standard accident police report includes the date, time, and location of the accident; names, contact information, driver's license numbers, and insurance information for all drivers; vehicle descriptions and damage summaries; a diagram of the accident scene; weather and road conditions; statements from drivers and witnesses; any citations issued; and the officer's observations and preliminary fault notation if made. Read each section carefully. Errors in names, addresses, insurance information, or the accident narrative can affect your claim and should be corrected promptly. Pay particular attention to the officer's narrative section — this is where any fault observations, contributing factors, and witness summaries are documented.
tips: Review the officer's narrative section — this is where fault observations are noted
Check all personal information, insurance details, and vehicle descriptions for accuracy
Note whether any citations were issued to either driver and for what violations

sections.heading: How to Correct Errors in a Police Report
sections.content: Police reports can contain factual errors — misspelled names, wrong vehicle descriptions, or inaccurate narratives. Contact the reporting officer or the agency's records division to request a correction or addendum. In California and Arizona, agencies can add a supplemental report or make corrections to factual errors. You generally cannot change the officer's subjective observations or fault notation, but factual errors are correctable. If you believe the report significantly misrepresents the accident, your attorney can submit a written dispute to the file that becomes part of the official record. Acting quickly matters — the longer you wait, the harder corrections become.
tips: Contact the records division of the reporting agency to request factual corrections
Corrections are typically made as addendums, not changes to the original document
An attorney can submit a written letter disputing significant factual inaccuracies

sections.heading: Using the Police Report in Your Claim
sections.content: Once you have the police report, use it proactively. Compare the officer's account of the accident to your own recollection and to any photos or witness statements you have gathered. If the report supports your version of events — if citations were issued to the other driver, if the officer's diagram shows the other vehicle at fault, if witness statements align with your account — reference these specifically in any communication with the insurance adjuster. If the report contains an error or inaccuracy that works against you, document the correction you have requested and provide your own written account of the accident to your insurer or attorney.
tips: Identify every element of the report that supports your account and reference it in claim communications
If the report contains errors, document your correction request in writing and follow up
Share the police report with your attorney at or before the first consultation

sections.heading: What to Do If Police Did Not Respond to Your Accident
sections.content: In some accidents — particularly minor collisions in parking lots, low-speed fender-benders, or incidents on private property — police may not respond or may decline to file a report. In these situations, creating your own documentation becomes even more critical. Photograph the scene, exchange information, gather witness contact details, and write a detailed written account of what happened as soon as possible. In California, if no officer filed a report, drivers involved in an accident causing injury or property damage over $1,000 must file a Report of Traffic Accident with the DMV within 10 days (Form SR-1). Arizona has a similar requirement. These driver-filed reports provide at least a basic official record for insurance purposes.
tips: California requires a DMV SR-1 report within 10 days if police did not respond and damages exceed $1,000
Your own written account of the accident, signed and dated, serves as documentation if no official report exists
Witness statements and photographs are especially important when there is no police report

sections.heading: Supplementing the Police Report With Your Own Documentation
sections.content: No matter how thorough the police report, it is a starting point — not the complete picture of your accident. Officers typically arrive after the fact, may spend limited time at the scene, and write reports based on incomplete information gathered quickly. Your own documentation supplements the report: your photographs capture angles the officer may not have noted; your written account records details that did not make the report; your witness contacts preserve testimony that the officer's brief canvas may have missed. Think of the police report and your own documentation as complementary — together they form a stronger record than either alone.
tips: Your photos, written account, and witness contacts supplement what the police report may have missed
The officer's scene diagram may be simplified — your photos show the actual positions more accurately
A detailed written account from you, written the same day as the accident, is valuable evidence

sections.heading: The Police Report and Your Overall Evidence File
sections.content: The police report is one document in a broader evidence file that supports your insurance claim or lawsuit. Think of it as the official starting point — authoritative on basic facts, but incomplete. Your own evidence supplements it: your photographs capture angles and conditions that the officer may not have noted; your written account records details that were not part of the brief scene investigation; your witness contacts preserve testimony that the officer's quick canvas may have missed. When you review the police report, compare it against your own documentation and note any significant differences. If the report contains errors that affect fault allocation or the factual record, pursue corrections promptly through the agency's records process. If the report broadly supports your account — if citations were issued to the other driver, if witness statements corroborate your version — reference those specifically in your insurance claim communications.
tips: The police report is a starting point, not the complete evidence record
Compare the report to your own photos and written account and note any discrepancies
Reference favorable elements of the police report specifically in insurance claim communications
